name: Katha Upanishad
summary: The Katha Upanishad presents the dialogue between young Nachiketa and Yama, the god of death. Nachiketa asks for the secret of death and immortality. Yama reveals the nature of the Atman — smaller than the smallest, greater than the greatest — and the path to liberation through discrimination and self-control.

verses.sanskrit:
उशन् ह वै वाजश्रवसः सर्ववेदसं ददौ |
तस्य ह नचिकेता नाम पुत्र आस ||१.१.१||
verses.transliteration:
uśan ha vai vājaśravasaḥ sarvavedasaṃ dadau |
tasya ha naciketā nāma putra āsa ||1.1.1||
verses.english_translation: Vajashravasa, desiring the fruit of the sacrifice, gave away all his possessions. He had a son named Nachiketa.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: वाजश्रवसः — वाजश्रवसः पुत्रः — सर्ववेदसं सर्वस्वं ददौ — विश्वजिद्यागे।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Vajashravasa, son of Vajashravas, gave away all his wealth in the Vishvajit sacrifice, desiring its fruits. His son Nachiketa observed this.

verses.sanskrit: स होवाच पितरं तत कस्मै मां दास्यसीति | द्वितीयं तृतीयं | तं होवाच मृत्यवे त्वा ददामीति ||१.१.२||
verses.transliteration: sa hovāca pitaraṃ tata kasmai māṃ dāsyasīti | dvitīyaṃ tṛtīyaṃ | taṃ hovāca mṛtyave tvā dadāmīti ||1.1.2||

word_meanings.meaning: Father, to whom will you give me?

verses.english_translation: He said to his father: 'Father, to whom will you give me?' A second and third time. He said to him: 'I give you to Death.'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नचिकेताः पितरम् अपृच्छत् — कस्मै माम् दास्यसि। पिता क्रुद्धः — मृत्यवे त्वा ददामि — इत्युक्तवान्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa, seeing his father give away old and barren cows, asked three times to whom he would give his son. The father, irritated, said 'I give you to Death.' Nachiketa, taking his father's word seriously, went to Yama's abode.

verses.english_translation: Verse 3 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.

verses.sanskrit: नचिकेता उवाच | अनशनन्त्रिरात्रमतिथिर्ब्राह्मणो गृहे | तस्यानशनेन किं स्यादिति चिन्तयन् ||१.१.७||
verses.transliteration: naciketā uvāca | anaśnan trirātram atithir brāhmaṇo gṛhe | tasyānaśanena kiṃ syād iti cintayan ||1.1.7||
verses.english_translation: Nachiketa said: 'A brahmin guest fasting for three nights in a house — what happens by his fasting?'
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: नचिकेताः त्रिरात्रम् अनशन् — यमस्य गृहे उपवासम् अकरोत्। ब्राह्मणस्य अतिथेः अनशनेन गृहस्थस्य पापम् भवति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa waited at Yama's door for three nights without food. When Yama returned, he was told that a brahmin guest had been fasting — which brings harm to the host. Yama offered three boons to compensate.

verses.sanskrit: तिस्रो रात्रीर्यदवात्सीर्गृहे मेऽनश्नन् ब्रह्मन् | नमस्तेऽस्तु ब्रह्मन् स्वस्ति मेऽस्तु | तस्मात्प्रति त्रीन् वरान् वृणीष्व ||१.१.९||
verses.transliteration: tisro rātrīr yad avātsīr gṛhe me'naśnan brahman | namas te'stu brahman svasti me'stu | tasmāt prati trīn varān vṛṇīṣva ||1.1.9||
verses.english_translation: You stayed three nights in my house without eating, O brahmin. Salutation to you, O brahmin. May it be well with me. Therefore choose three boons.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: यमः नचिकेतसम् अब्रवीत् — त्रिरात्रम् अनश्नन् अवात्सीः — तस्मात् त्रीन् वरान् वृणीष्व।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Yama, the lord of death, honored Nachiketa and offered three boons as compensation for the three nights of fasting. This shows that even Yama respects a sincere brahmin seeker.

verses.sanskrit: शान्तसंकल्पः सुमना यथा स्याद्वीतमन्युर्गौतमो माभि मृत्यो | त्वत्प्रसृष्टं माभिवदेत्प्रतीत | एतत्त्रयाणां प्रथमं वरं वृणे ||१.१.१०||
verses.transliteration: śāntasaṃkalpaḥ sumanā yathā syād vītamanyur gautamo mābhi mṛtyo | tvat prasṛṣṭaṃ mābhi vadet pratīta | etat trayāṇāṃ prathamaṃ varaṃ vṛṇe ||1.1.10||

word_meanings.meaning: of peaceful mind and good disposition

verses.english_translation: May my father Gautama be of peaceful mind and good disposition, free from anger toward me, O Death. May he recognize me when released by you. This I choose as the first of the three boons.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रथमः वरः — पितुः शान्तिः। नचिकेताः पितरम् प्रथमम् स्मरति — पितृभक्तिम् दर्शयति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa's first boon is for his father's peace of mind. This shows Nachiketa's character — even before asking about immortality, he thinks of his father's welfare. Yama grants this boon immediately.

verses.sanskrit: स्वर्गे लोके न भयं किञ्चनास्ति न तत्र त्वं न जरया बिभेति | उभे तीर्त्वाऽशनायापिपासे शोकातिगो मोदते स्वर्गलोके ||१.१.१२||
verses.transliteration: svarge loke na bhayaṃ kiñcanāsti na tatra tvaṃ na jarayā bibheti | ubhe tīrtvā'śanāyāpipāse śokātigo modate svargaloke ||1.1.12||

word_meanings.meaning: in the heavenly world there is no fear

verses.english_translation: In the heavenly world there is no fear whatsoever. You are not there, nor does one fear old age. Having crossed both hunger and thirst, beyond grief, one rejoices in the heavenly world.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: स्वर्गे — देवलोके — भयम् नास्ति। जरा नास्ति। क्षुत्पिपासे नास्ते। शोकः नास्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa describes the heavenly world as free from fear, old age, hunger, thirst, and grief. He asks for the Nachiketa fire (the ritual that leads to heaven) as his second boon. Yama grants this and names the fire after Nachiketa.

verses.sanskrit: येयं प्रेते विचिकित्सा मनुष्येऽस्तीत्येके नायमस्तीति चैके | एतद्विद्यामनुशिष्टस्त्वयाऽहं वराणामेष वरस्तृतीयः ||१.१.२०||
verses.transliteration: yeyaṃ prete vicikitsā manuṣye'stīty eke nāyam astīti caike | etad vidyām anuśiṣṭas tvayā'haṃ varāṇām eṣa varas tṛtīyaḥ ||1.1.20||

word_meanings.meaning: others say he does not exist

verses.english_translation: There is this doubt about a person who has died — some say 'he exists,' others say 'he does not exist.' I wish to be taught this by you. This is the third of the boons.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: प्रेते — मृते — विचिकित्सा — संशयः। अस्ति इति — आत्मा अस्ति। न अस्ति इति — आत्मा नास्ति। एतत् विद्याम् — इदम् ज्ञातुम् इच्छामि।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa's third boon is the most profound — the secret of death and the existence of the Self after death. This is the central question of the Katha Upanishad. Yama tries to dissuade Nachiketa with wealth and pleasures, but Nachiketa insists.

verses.sanskrit: न वित्तेन तर्पणीयो मनुष्यो लप्स्यामहे वित्तमद्राक्ष्म चेत्त्वा | जीविष्यामो यावदीशिष्यसि त्वं वरस्तु मे वरणीयः स एव ||१.१.२७||
verses.transliteration: na vittena tarpaṇīyo manuṣyo lapsyāmahe vittam adrākṣma cet tvā | jīviṣyāmo yāvad īśiṣyasi tvaṃ varas tu me varaṇīyaḥ sa eva ||1.1.27||

word_meanings.meaning: a person cannot be satisfied by wealth

verses.english_translation: A person cannot be satisfied by wealth. We shall obtain wealth when we have seen you. We shall live as long as you rule. But the boon to be chosen by me is that very one.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: न वित्तेन तर्पणीयः — धनेन मनुष्यः न तृप्यति। वरः मे वरणीयः स एव — मृत्युरहस्यम् एव वृणे।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Nachiketa refuses all of Yama's temptations — wealth, women, kingdoms, long life. He says a person cannot be satisfied by wealth. The only boon worth choosing is the knowledge of the Self. This shows Nachiketa's supreme vairagya (dispassion).

verses.sanskrit: श्रेयश्च प्रेयश्च मनुष्यमेतः | तौ सम्परीत्य विविनक्ति धीरः | श्रेयो हि धीरोऽभि प्रेयसो वृणीते | प्रेयो मन्दो योगक्षेमाद्वृणीते ||१.२.२||
verses.transliteration: śreyaś ca preyaś ca manuṣyam etaḥ | tau samparītya vivinakti dhīraḥ | śreyo hi dhīro'bhi preyaso vṛṇīte | preyo mando yogakṣemād vṛṇīte ||1.2.2||
verses.english_translation: Both the good and the pleasant approach a person. The wise one examines them and discriminates. The wise one chooses the good over the pleasant. The foolish one chooses the pleasant for the sake of comfort.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: श्रेयः — मोक्षसाधनम्। प्रेयः — भोगसाधनम्। धीरः — विवेकी — श्रेयः वृणीते। मन्दः — अविवेकी — प्रेयः वृणीते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Shreyas is what leads to the highest good (liberation); preyas is what is merely pleasant (sense enjoyment). The wise discriminate between them and choose the good. The foolish choose the pleasant. Nachiketa chose shreyas — the knowledge of the Self.

verses.sanskrit: आत्मानं रथिनं विद्धि शरीरं रथमेव तु | बुद्धिं तु सारथिं विद्धि मनः प्रग्रहमेव च ||१.३.३||
verses.transliteration: ātmānaṃ rathinaṃ viddhi śarīraṃ ratham eva tu | buddhiṃ tu sārathiṃ viddhi manaḥ pragraham eva ca ||1.3.3||

word_meanings.meaning: know the Self as the rider of the chariot

verses.english_translation: Know the Self as the rider of the chariot, the body as the chariot, the intellect as the charioteer, and the mind as the reins.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: आत्मा — रथी — भोक्ता। शरीरम् — रथः — भोगसाधनम्। बुद्धिः — सारथिः — निर्णायिका। मनः — प्रग्रहः — संकल्पविकल्पात्मकम्।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: This famous chariot analogy describes the relationship between the Self, body, intellect, mind, and senses. The Self is the passenger; the body is the vehicle; the intellect is the driver; the mind is the reins; the senses are the horses; the sense objects are the roads.

verses.transliteration: indriyāṇi hayān āhur viṣayāṃs teṣu gocarān | ātmendriyamanoyuktaṃ bhoktety āhur manīṣiṇaḥ ||1.3.4||

word_meanings.meaning: the Self joined with senses and mind

verses.english_translation: The senses are called the horses; the sense objects are their roads. The Self joined with the senses and mind — the wise call it the enjoyer.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: इन्द्रियाणि — हयाः — विषयेषु धावन्ति। विषयाः — गोचराः — मार्गाः। आत्मा इन्द्रियमनोयुक्तः — भोक्ता — संसारी।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The senses are like horses that run toward their objects. The sense objects are the roads they travel. The Self, when identified with the senses and mind, becomes the enjoyer — the bound individual soul in samsara.

verses.sanskrit: यस्त्वविज्ञानवान् भवत्ययुक्तेन मनसा सदा | तस्येन्द्रियाण्यवश्यानि दुष्टाश्वा इव सारथेः ||१.३.५||
verses.transliteration: yas tv avijñānavān bhavaty ayuktena manasā sadā | tasyendriyāṇy avaśyāni duṣṭāśvā iva sārtheḥ ||1.3.5||
verses.english_translation: One who is without discrimination, with always uncontrolled mind — his senses are uncontrolled, like vicious horses of a charioteer.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अविज्ञानवान् — विवेकरहितः — अयुक्तेन मनसा — असंयतचित्तेन — तस्य इन्द्रियाणि अवश्यानि — दुष्टाश्वाः इव।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: Without discrimination (viveka) and an uncontrolled mind, the senses run wild like vicious horses. Such a person is dragged through samsara. The solution is to develop discrimination and control the mind through the reins of the intellect.

verses.sanskrit: तद्विष्णोः परमं पदं सदा पश्यन्ति सूरयः | दिवीव चक्षुरातातम् ||१.३.९||
verses.transliteration: tad viṣṇoḥ paramaṃ padaṃ sadā paśyanti sūrayaḥ | divīva cakṣur ātatam ||1.3.9||

word_meanings.meaning: like an eye spread across the sky

verses.english_translation: The wise always see the supreme abode of Vishnu, spread out like an eye across the sky.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: विष्णोः परमम् पदम् — परब्रह्मणः उत्तमम् स्थानम् — सूरयः — ज्ञानिनः — सदा पश्यन्ति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The supreme abode of Vishnu (Brahman) is always seen by the wise — not with physical eyes but with the eye of knowledge. It is spread everywhere like the sky, and the wise perceive it in all things.

verses.sanskrit: न जायते म्रियते वा विपश्चिन्नायं कुतश्चिन्न बभूव कश्चित् | अजो नित्यः शाश्वतोऽयं पुराणो न हन्यते हन्यमाने शरीरे ||१.२.१८||
verses.transliteration: na jāyate mriyate vā vipaścin nāyaṃ kutaścin na babhūva kaścit | ajo nityaḥ śāśvato'yaṃ purāṇo na hanyate hanyamāne śarīre ||1.2.18||

word_meanings.meaning: is not killed when the body is killed

verses.english_translation: The wise one is not born, nor does it die. It did not come from anywhere, nor did it become anything. Unborn, eternal, everlasting, ancient — it is not killed when the body is killed.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: न जायते — उत्पत्तिरहितः। न म्रियते — विनाशरहितः। अजः — जन्मरहितः। नित्यः — कालत्रयेऽपि अस्ति। न हन्यते — शरीरनाशेऽपि आत्मा न नश्यति।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is beyond birth and death. It was not born, it does not die, it has no origin and no end. It is unborn, eternal, ancient, and everlasting. When the body is destroyed, the Self is not destroyed — for the Self is not the body. This verse also appears in the Bhagavad Gita (2.20).

verses.english_translation: Verse 16 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.
Verse 17 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.
Verse 18 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.
Verse 19 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.

verses.sanskrit:
अणोरणीयान् महतो महीयानात्मास्य जन्तोर्निहितो गुहायाम् |
तमक्रतुः पश्यति वीतशोको धातुप्रसादान्महिमानमात्मनः ||१.२.२०||
verses.transliteration:
aṇor aṇīyān mahato mahīyān ātmāsya jantor nihito guhāyām |
tam akratuḥ paśyati vītaśoko dhātuprasādān mahimānam ātmanaḥ ||1.2.20||

word_meanings.meaning: hidden in the cave (of the heart)
by the grace of the Creator

verses.english_translation: The Self, smaller than the smallest, greater than the greatest, is hidden in the heart of this creature. One who is free from desire and grief, by the grace of the Creator, sees the glory of the Self.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: अणोः अणीयान् — परमाणोरपि सूक्ष्मतरः। महतः महीयान् — महत्तत्त्वादपि महत्तरः। गुहायाम् — हृदयाकाशे — निहितः।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self is subtler than the subtlest atom and vaster than the vastest. It is hidden in the cave of the heart. Only one who is free from desire and sorrow, by the grace of the Lord, can perceive the glory of the Self.

verses.english_translation: Verse 21 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.
Verse 22 of the Katha Upanishad. Full content will be added.

verses.sanskrit:
नायमात्मा प्रवचनेन लभ्यो न मेधया न बहुना श्रुतेन |
यमेवैष वृणुते तेन लभ्यस्तस्यैष आत्मा विवृणुते तनूं स्वाम् ||१.२.२३||
verses.transliteration:
nāyam ātmā pravacanena labhyo na medhayā na bahunā śrutena |
yam evaisa vṛṇute tena labhyas tasyaiṣa ātmā vivṛṇute tanūṃ svām ||1.2.23||

word_meanings.meaning: by that one it is attained

verses.english_translation: This Self cannot be attained by instruction, nor by intellect, nor by much learning. It is attained by the one whom it chooses — to that one the Self reveals its own form.
verses.shankara_bhashya_sanskrit: न प्रवचनेन — बहुशास्त्राध्ययनेन न लभ्यः। न मेधया — तर्कशक्त्या न गृह्यते। यम् एव एषः वृणुते — यस्मिन् प्रसीदति — तस्मै स्वयं प्रकाशते।
verses.shankara_bhashya_english: Shankara: The Self cannot be known through mere study of scriptures, nor through intellectual reasoning, nor through vast learning. It reveals itself only to the one it chooses — the one in whom it is pleased. This grace comes through sincere devotion and purity of heart.
